lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market certificated carriers that repeatedly operate scheduled freight routes for shippers, logistics networks, integrators or public-sector customers, including independent feeder airlines under recurring service agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Passenger-only airlines, nonscheduled charter cargo flying, freight forwarders without air-carrier operations, ground handling and MRO-only businesses, captive internal flight departments, dormant certificate shells and financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue comes from scheduled air-freight charges or recurring capacity, dry-lease and feeder-service contracts; some contracts reimburse specified direct operating costs and pay aircraft- or route-linked administrative compensation.

a.bridge: BLS employment data cover all scheduled air transportation rather than six-digit scheduled freight, while IATA adoption data cover the global air-cargo ecosystem rather than US LMM carriers. I treat cargo documentation, booking, tracking, exception handling, compliance preparation, network planning and administrative work as partly AI-exposed, but exclude most flying, loading and hands-on maintenance. The proxy may overstate new exposure because electronic air waybills and workflow automation are already widely deployed.
a.rationale: BLS counts 9,440 cargo and freight agents in scheduled air transportation, and the broader industry also contains large pilot, maintenance and material-moving workforces. IATA says electronic air waybills are used for more than two-thirds of shipments and ONE Record supports automated digital services, showing a meaningful information-work surface but also substantial prior digitization.
a.change_evidence: Payroll and task logs from at least five LMM scheduled cargo carriers, separated into flight, maintenance, ramp, dispatch, cargo-documentation, customer, compliance and corporate work and tagged for current automation, would materially tighten the estimate.
caveats: The BLS scheduled-air-transportation occupation mix is dominated by passenger carriers.
IATA adoption figures are global and weighted toward air-waybill volume, not small US operators.
The estimate is for not-yet-automated tasks and therefore discounts existing e-AWB and rules-based automation.

rho.rationale: Standardized shipment data and active ONE Record implementations make document, tracking and planning tools feasible within five years. Implementation remains limited by fragmented counterparties, legacy carrier systems, cybersecurity, customer approvals and FAA safety assurance for operational uses; the IATA 2025 survey found nearly 50% of respondents ready for ONE Record rather than universal readiness.
rho.change_evidence: Carrier implementation studies reporting production adoption, workflow coverage, exception rates, labor hours removed and safety/compliance incidents for ONE Record and AI tools would materially move this estimate.
caveats: ONE Record readiness is not a direct measure of generative-AI implementation.
Small carriers may depend on an integrator's systems and permission to change workflows.
Safety-critical deployment has a higher assurance burden than administrative deployment.

e.rationale: Active independent scheduled freight and feeder carriers fit the recurring external-customer lens, but the injected 25-firm band estimate can include mixed passenger/cargo airlines, dormant certificates, captive subsidiaries, nonscheduled operations or firms whose economics reside in adjacent MRO and logistics activities. FAA's reporting list includes many carrier types, while Air T demonstrates that distinct feeder airlines can have LMM-sized EBITDA yet be embedded in a diversified parent and overwhelmingly dependent on one integrator.
e.change_evidence: A certificate-by-certificate US census joined to scheduled freight revenue, ownership, customer contracts, aircraft activity and normalized standalone EBITDA would directly identify the eligible population.
caveats: The injected firm count is an ESTIMATE based on size classes and an industry margin, not a verified list of 25 scheduled cargo carriers.
FAA all-cargo reporting includes foreign, scheduled and nonscheduled carriers beyond the lens.
A subsidiary may be operationally coherent but unavailable as a standalone control acquisition.

s5.rationale: Cargo-airline control transfers are possible but uncommon and must preserve certificates, scarce pilots, aircraft, safety systems and anchor contracts. Ameriflight's acquisition of Wiggins is direct but old evidence, while the broad small-business market recorded 9,586 transfers in 2025; neither provides an industry denominator or five-year probability.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of ownership changes, mergers, bankruptcies, asset sales and failed sale processes among US scheduled freight certificate holders, with viable control transfers distinguished from reorganizations, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The direct carrier acquisition is from 2014 and market structure has since changed.
Broad small-business transaction data are not representative of regulated airlines.
Customer consent or termination rights may prevent a nominal equity sale from transferring durable cash flow.

q.bridge: Air T's filed FedEx feeder contracts directly show cost reimbursement and termination mechanics for two US overnight cargo operators, but not the commercial-retention share of implemented AI savings across all LMM scheduled freight carriers. I infer low-to-moderate retention because reimbursed costs have no markup, administrative compensation depends on aircraft deployed and the dominant customer can rapidly reduce routes. This may understate retention for diversified carriers selling directly to shippers.
q.rationale: Air T reports that fuel, landing, third-party maintenance, parts and certain operating costs are reimbursed without markup and that FedEx may reduce aircraft on ten days' notice. FAA also states that cargo carriers face price competition from truck, ship, rail and other air carriers, making full retention unlikely.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level analysis of fee formulas, cost pass-through, productivity-sharing clauses and renewal repricing across direct cargo and feeder carriers, followed by realized margin data after automation, would materially move this value.
caveats: One public parent's FedEx feeder contracts may not represent direct shipper pricing.
Cost reimbursement can protect downside while also preventing retention of savings on reimbursed lines.
This primitive excludes volume loss and implementation failure.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts domestic and total US commercial-carrier revenue ton miles, including passenger belly freight and carriers outside the LMM lens, rather than constant-quality demand for eligible scheduled freight operators. I translate the 2.1% annual domestic cargo RTM forecast into a five-year base near 1.11 and widen for trade, modal-shift, customer-concentration and e-commerce uncertainty. This may overstate small-carrier demand if growth consolidates in integrator networks or passenger belly capacity.
d5.rationale: FAA forecasts domestic cargo RTMs to grow 2.1% annually from 2026 to 2046 and says all-cargo carriers handled 93.3% of domestic cargo RTMs in 2025. It also identifies GDP, trade, fuel, yields and modal competition as key demand drivers.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year customer and route forecasts for the eligible carriers, split by integrator feeder, postal, express, industrial and international traffic and adjusted for real yields and mode shift, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Revenue ton miles combine quantity and route length and are an imperfect constant-quality service measure.
The FAA forecast is systemwide rather than LMM-carrier-specific.
Trade restrictions, recession, fuel shocks and shifts to ground transport can sharply change demand.

o.rationale: Almost all scheduled air-freight quantity still requires a legally accountable aircraft operator over five years. Digital booking, records and tracking can remove transactions and administrative touches but cannot itself operate the aircraft, maintain airworthiness or assume carrier safety obligations.
o.change_evidence: FAA approval and scaled commercial use of remotely piloted or autonomous cargo aircraft under scheduled-service certificates, together with evidence that software platforms assume operational control, would materially reduce the estimate.
caveats: Automation can eliminate pieces of the current service basket while leaving the carrier accountable.
Small unmanned cargo aircraft may emerge first on thin routes but face certification and integration barriers.
The estimate refers to operator-required transport quantity, not paperwork or booking contacts.

narrative.executive_view: Scheduled freight aviation has a meaningful information-work opportunity because shipment data, compliance records, routing and exceptions are increasingly digital. The physical operation remains regulated and labor-intensive, while feeder economics are often concentrated in powerful customers that can pass through costs and capture savings.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate cargo-document validation, booking, tracking, customs and dangerous-goods checks, irregular-operations triage, maintenance planning and administrative work. Piloting, loading, inspections, repairs and final operational accountability remain largely outside substitution over five years.
narrative.value_capture: Direct-to-shipper carriers face competing modes and other airlines; feeder carriers may operate under reimbursement and aircraft-linked fee contracts with short reduction rights. Savings can improve reliability and protect margins, but customers are positioned to reprice or reduce capacity.
narrative.firm_availability: Some independent feeder operators clearly fit the recurring-service lens, yet the apparent 25-firm pool needs verification for active schedules, independence and standalone EBITDA. A viable acquisition must transfer both regulatory capability and an anchor contract that may itself be terminable.
narrative.demand_durability: FAA projects moderate domestic air-cargo growth and all-cargo aircraft dominate domestic RTMs. Demand is derived from economic activity and exposed to trade, fuel, real yields, e-commerce patterns and substitution by ground or ocean modes.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal uncertainties are the true eligible firm population, existing automation, integrator concentration and the share of savings retained under customer contracts. Cyber failures, safety incidents, pilot costs and contract termination could overwhelm incremental productivity gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Digitally structured cargo data and recurring network operations create a practical AI surface while physical transport still needs a certificated operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Anchor-customer concentration and reimbursement-style contracts sharply constrain transferability and retained economics.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS occupation-industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Reports 9,440 cargo and freight agents employed in scheduled air transportation.

sources.title: IATA ONE Record
sources.supports: States that e-air-waybills are used for more than two out of three shipments and describes standardized shipment data APIs enabling automated digital cargo services.

sources.title: IATA Survey Shows Growing Awareness of ONE Record and Areas for Further Support
sources.supports: Reports over 70% awareness, nearly 50% readiness and more than 30 active ONE Record pilots among surveyed cargo participants.

sources.title: Technical Discipline: Artificial Intelligence – Machine Learning
sources.supports: FAA explains that aviation AI/ML functionality and performance must be evaluated within the certification framework to assure safety.

sources.title: Air T, Inc. 2026 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes two US overnight feeder airlines, their long FedEx relationship, cost reimbursement without markup, rapid customer termination and aircraft-reduction rights, and LMM-sized carrier EBITDA.

sources.title: Cargo Carriers for Reporting Cargo Data to the FAA, CY 2025
sources.supports: Provides the current FAA reporting roster and illustrates that the all-cargo carrier universe contains many US and foreign operator types requiring lens-level cleaning.

sources.title: Regularly Scheduled Air Carriers (Part 121)
sources.supports: States that Part 121 certificate holders include all-cargo operators and requires approved hazardous-materials programs and employee training.

sources.title: Ameriflight LLC Acquires Wiggins Airways
sources.supports: Documents the closed acquisition of a US regional overnight air-cargo carrier, direct evidence that control transfer can occur.

sources.title: 2025 Year in Review: BizBuySell Market Recap
sources.supports: Reports 9,586 US small-business transactions in 2025; used only as broad transaction-market context, not an airline rate.

sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast Fiscal Years 2026–2046
sources.supports: Forecasts domestic cargo RTMs to grow 2.1% annually from 2026 to 2046, reports all-cargo carriers at 93.3% of 2025 domestic cargo RTMs and describes competition from alternative modes.

sources.title: UPS to become the primary air cargo provider for the United States Postal Service
sources.supports: Reports a minimum five-and-a-half-year USPS air-cargo contract with UPS and the displacement of FedEx, illustrating durable contracted demand and renewal displacement risk.
